original_abstract: While many American voters express a combination of conservative and liberal positions across different policy areas, the nature of these viewpoints remains a subject of intense scholarly debate. Conventional wisdom suggests these voters are "cross-pressured," yet a recent and influential study by Fowler et al. (2023) argues that such individuals are actually centrists who hold moderate positions on specific issues. This conclusion rests on a novel methodological framework designed to determine whether a voter’s preferences are accurately captured by a traditional liberal-conservative ideological scale. Fowler et al. find that most voters fit this ideological model, implying that those with mid-range ideological scores must hold moderate policy views. In this paper, we demonstrate that this method systematically overestimates the ideological consistency of the electorate. The framework incorrectly classifies voters as ideologically coherent unless their responses are either entirely random or form a distinct, non-conforming cluster. Through simulations, we show that this methodological bias is substantial. Consequently, the claim that voters with mixed political profiles are actually centrist policy supporters remains unsupported, leaving the "cross-pressured" explanation as a vital alternative.
humanized_abstract: Voters in the United States frequently express conservative and liberal opinions on different issues. However, there is disagreement in the literature about what those opinions mean. A significant number of studies interpret such voters as being “cross-pressured,” which implies that their political views are at odds with one another. However, in a recent paper, Fowler et al. (2023) argue instead that such voters are centrists with moderate views on specific issues. They propose an innovative way of analyzing the views of voters and determine that the vast majority of voters can be classified as being on the conventional liberal–conservative ideological scale. They then argue that centrist voters with moderate views must be those with mid-bunch scores. We show in this paper that their method of analysis overstates the extent to which voters have ideological consistent opinions. The method only fails to classify a voter as being ideologically consistent if her opinions are either randomly assigned or fall outside of the main cluster. We show through simulations that the bias is substantial and, therefore, that Fowler et al. fail to show that cross-pressured voters are centrists with moderate policy views.

They propose an innovative way of analyzing the views of voters and determine that the vast majority of voters can be classified as being on the conventional liberal–conservative ideological scale. They then argue that centrist voters with moderate views must be those with mid-bunch scores. We show in this paper that their method of analysis overstates the extent to which voters have ideological consistent opinions. The method only fails to classify a voter as being ideologically consistent if her opinions are either randomly assigned or fall outside of the main cluster. We show through simulations that the bias is substantial and, therefore, that Fowler et al. fail to show that cross-pressured voters are centrists with moderate policy views.
